does: Red-Bandit develops a test-time adaptive red-teaming framework that combines parameter-efficient LoRA experts with multi-armed bandit selection to efficiently identify LLM vulnerabilities. The method post-trains multiple LoRA experts, each specialized for a distinct attack style such as manipulation or slang, using reinforcement learning that rewards unsafe prompt generation guided by a rule-based safety model. At inference time, a bandit algorithm dynamically selects among these experts based on the target model's safety responses, balancing exploration and exploitation to discover failure modes. The approach achieves state-of-the-art attack success rates on AdvBench benchmarks while generating more human-readable adversarial prompts with lower perplexity compared to existing methods.
connects: This work builds on the emerging area of automated red-teaming for LLMs, extending prior approaches that primarily operate in a static, non-adaptive manner. Red-Bandit combines several established techniques: parameter-efficient fine-tuning via LoRA, which has become standard for adapting large models; reinforcement learning for adversarial prompt generation as explored in existing red-teaming work; and multi-armed bandit algorithms from online learning literature. The framework responds to limitations in current red-teaming methods by adding online adaptation mechanisms, directly addressing the challenge that pre-computed attack strategies often fail to account for model-specific vulnerability patterns that become apparent only during inference.
enables: Red-Bandit opens several research directions for adaptive security evaluation of LLMs. The bandit policy itself becomes a diagnostic tool that reveals which attack styles are most effective against particular models, enabling more targeted model improvement and safety interventions. Future work can extend this framework to handle continuously evolving model defenses, develop hierarchical or learned bandit policies beyond simple multi-armed approaches, and integrate human feedback during test-time adaptation to make red-teaming more interactive. The approach also suggests broader applications for test-time adaptation in adversarial settings, potentially informing how adaptive attacks and defenses might coevolve, and establishing foundations for more efficient auditing pipelines that don't require retraining for each new model variant.
